statement.p: It takes one painter 15\ {\rm hr} longer to paint a house than it does a more experienced painter. Together they can paint the house in 22\ {\rm hr}. How long does it take for each painter to paint the house working alone?
The more experienced painter takes to paint the house alone, and the less experienced painter takes .

solution.p: Lets understand how to use fractions to model work problems. Assume it takes a person 5 hours to complete a job. If we treat the whole job as one whole, each hour, this person would complete \frac{1}{5} of the job.
Similarly, if it takes a person x hours to complete a job, each hour, this person would complete \frac{1}{x} of the job.
Now we are ready to model this situation with math.
Assume that, if the experienced painter paints the house alone, it would take x hours to complete the job. Each hour, the experiended painter does \frac{1}{x} of the whole job.

Its given that, if the slower painter works alone, it would take 15 more hours to complete the job. So it would take the slower painter x+15 hours to complete the job. Each hour, the slower painter does \frac{1}{x+15} of the whole job.
Its given that, if they work together, it took them 22 hours to complete the job. Each hour, they can complete \frac{1}{22} of the whole job.
Now we can write an equation and solve it:
In the second step, to get rid of all fractions, we multiplied each term with the common denominator {22x\mathopen{}\left(x+15\right)}.
When numbers are big, we use the quadratic formula to solve the quadratic equation:
In this situation, x=-8.74 is not a valid solution, since hours cannot be negative. x=37.74 is the only solution. It would take the more experienced painter 37.74 hours to complete the job.
Since, if the less experienced painter works alone, it would take 15 more hours to complete the job. So it would take the less experienced painter 37.74+15=52.74 hours to complete the job.
